title: Applying exterior wood stain or oil to garden joinery
subtitle: Clean, prepare, and apply a penetrating wood stain or oil to a fence, deck, or gate
excerpt: How to apply exterior wood stain or decking oil to outdoor timber. Covers cleaning down weathered wood, the correct preparation for bare or previously stained surfaces, and how to apply so the finish penetrates evenly.
sourceNotes: Ronseal technical guidance (generic guidance on penetrating stains, referenced not reproduced). Traditional UK exterior woodwork practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: A spirit-based or water-based finish that soaks into the wood fibres rather than forming a surface film. Because it has no surface film to crack or peel, it is the preferred exterior finish for garden timber that expands and contracts with weather.

A linseed or tung-oil-based treatment for decking, fences, and gates. Penetrates the timber and feeds the wood fibres, increasing weather resistance. Applied by brush or roller; re-applied annually or biannually depending on exposure.

content.text: Exterior timber that is left untreated greys and splits within a few seasons as the sun bleaches out the lignin and moisture cycles check the wood surface. A
feeds the wood and slows the process. Both are easier to maintain than film-forming paints or varnishes because there is no surface film to peel off.

Apply in dry weather when the timber surface is completely dry throughout. A 48-hour dry spell before application is the minimum. Direct strong sun is not ideal; the stain skins too fast on very hot timber and penetrates less deeply. Early morning or a cloudy warm day gives the best result.

Scrub off any algae, mould, or lichen with a stiff brush and a solution of proprietary wood cleaner or a dilute bleach solution (1 part bleach to 10 parts water). Rinse with clean water and leave to dry fully, at least 2 days. Scrape off any flaking old stain with the paint scraper. On previously stained timber, use a wood stain remover if the old coat is incompatible with the new product (check the label). Sand the surface lightly with 80-grit paper on the sanding block to open the wood grain and remove loose fibres.

Stir the product before use (do not shake, shaking raises bubbles that dry on the surface). Apply liberally with the 50 mm brush, working with the grain. Apply enough to saturate the surface, a wet coat that stays looking wet for 10 to 15 minutes is about right. Work along the length of each board or rail without leaving dry brush marks at the edges.

Wait for the first coat to dry according to the label instructions (typically 4 to 6 hours for spirit-based, 2 to 4 hours for water-based). Apply the second coat in the same way. On previously untreated or very dry timber, the second coat penetrates more deeply and gives a richer colour. On re-treatment of previously stained timber, one coat may be sufficient, apply the second only if the first coat has been fully absorbed.

Decking: use a decking oil or a dedicated exterior decking stain. Decking surfaces benefit from the paint roller with a medium-pile sleeve for larger areas; a brush along the board edges after rolling keeps the application even.

Natural oil on hardwood: teak, iroko, and ipe benefit from a pure tung or linseed oil rather than a coloured stain. Apply thinly, wipe off excess within 20 minutes, and allow full cure (at least 3 days) before the second coat.
Dispose of oil-soaked cloths and brushes carefully, linseed-oil-soaked rags can self-ignite if left in a pile. Lay them flat outdoors to dry before disposal.

items.symptom: Blotchy finish with pale and dark patches
items.cause: Timber not uniformly dry at time of application, or old stain not fully removed
items.fix: Leave to cure fully, then sand lightly and apply another coat once the timber is uniformly dry.

items.cause: Film-forming varnish or paint underneath, the penetrating stain cannot bond through it
items.fix: Strip the old film-forming finish with a heat gun or stripper, sand to bare wood, and re-apply.
